This structure helps ensure that each section reflects the most relevant responses, but it also means that sample sizes and percentages can vary throughout the report.

The sections in this report provide a directional view of the SAP developer community in 2026, highlighting key patterns in developer roles, tools, learning habits, and technology adoption. In sections such as Developer Experience, the report focuses on workflows, environments, and friction points in day-to-day development. In Learning, it highlights how developers build skills, with strong reliance on tutorials, documentation, forums, and community resources. For example, the learning section shows that developers most often relied on developer community websites and forums (60%), online tutorials, how-to videos, or webinars (47%), and self-directed learning using documentation and sample code (41%).

Other References

The annual Stack Overflow Developer Survey is an excellent (and no-cost) reference for industry-wide habits of developers.

SlashData's Developer Nation Survey is also a good non-SAP resource.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-4]
entry.content:
Connecting CAP application to HANA Cloud and Deploying  to Cloud Foundry

In Part 3 , we created the SAP HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) on BTP and mapped it to our Cloud Foundry space.

In this Part, we will:

- Update package.json with the correct CDS profiles for each environment
- Run cds add hana to wire the CAP project for HANA Cloud deployment
- Build the MTA archive using mbt build
- Deploy the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry using cf deploy
- Verify the deployment and explore the HANA tables in DB Explorer
- Run the app locally in hybrid mode — connected to the deployed HANA instance

PREREQUISITES

Make sure the following tools are installed before proceeding:

- Cloud Foundry CLI (cf) — logged in to your BTP CF environment: cf login
- MBT (Multi-Target Application Build Tool): npm install -g mbt
- @sap/cds-dk (already installed from Part 1)


STEP 1 — UPDATE PACKAGE.JSON WITH CDS PROFILES

The package.json in the CAP project already has a "cds" section that defines how the app connects to the database in each environment. We need to make sure the three profiles are correctly configured:

development → uses SQLite (local, in-file database — for cds watch)
hybrid → uses SAP HANA Cloud (local app, cloud database — for testing)
production → uses SAP HANA Cloud (fully deployed on CF)

Update or verify the "cds" section in your package.json:

{
  "name": "incident-management",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Incident Management CAP Application",
  "repository": "<Add your repository here>",
  "license": "UNLICENSED",
  "private": true,
  "dependencies": {
    "@cap-js/hana": "^2",
    "@sap/cds": "^9",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^9",
    "@sap/xssec": "^4",
    "express": "^5"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@cap-js/sqlite": "^2",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^9",
    "cds-plugin-ui5": "^0.17.0"
  },
  "cds": {
    "requires": {
      "[development]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "sqlite"
        }
      },
      "[hybrid]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "hana-cloud"
        }
      },
      "[production]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "hana-cloud"
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "cds-serve",
    "watch": "cds watch",
    "watch-incidents": "cds watch --open incidents/index.html?sap-ui-xx-viewCache=false --livereload false"
  }
}

WHY THREE PROFILES?
This pattern lets you use the same codebase across all environments without changing any code — just switch the profile.

SQLite keeps local development fast and offline.

Hybrid lets you test against real HANA data without a full CF deployment.

Production is used when the app runs on Cloud Foundry.

 

STEP 2 — RUN cds add hana

Run the following command in the terminal from inside the project folder:

cds add hana

This single command does several important things to prepare your CAP project for SAP HANA Cloud deployment:

- Adds the @cap-js/hana dependency to package.json (HANA database adapter)
- Generates or updates the mta.yaml file with the correct MTA module definitions for CF deployment
- Adds an HDI container resource definition to mta.yaml
- Configures the production CDS profile to use hana-cloud as the db kind

After running this command, your mta.yaml will define three components:

1. incident-management-srv (Node.js module — the CAP OData service)
- Deployed as a CF application
- Provides: srv-api (the service URL consumed by the Fiori app later)
- Requires: incident-management-db (the HDI container)

2. incident-management-db-deployer (hdb module — the schema deployer)
- A one-time deployer app that pushes the CDS schema to HANA
- Runs during deployment then stops (Stopped state is normal)
- Requires: incident-management-db

3. incident-management-db (resource — the HDI container)
- Type: com.sap.xs.hdi-container
- Service: hana, Plan: hdi-shared
- This is the actual schema container created inside your HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) that was set up in Part 3 


STEP 3 — BUILD THE MTA ARCHIVE

Run the MBT build command to compile the project into a deployable archive:

mbt build

This command:
- Reads the mta.yaml configuration
- Runs `npm install` and `npx cds build --production` to compile the CDS model
- Packages all modules into a single .mtar archive file

The output archive is created at:

mta_archives/incident-management_1.0.0.mtar

 

STEP 4 — DEPLOY TO CLOUD FOUNDRY

Make sure you are logged in to CF and targeting the correct space:

cf login -a <api endpoint>
cf target -o <your-org> -s dev

Now deploy the MTA archive:

cf deploy mta_archives/incident-management_1.0.0.mtar

CF will:
1. Create the HDI container service instance (incident-management-db)
2. Deploy and run the db-deployer to push the schema to HANA
3. Start the CAP service application (incident-management-srv)

Once deployment completes, navigate to your CF space in BTP Cockpit:

Trial Home →   f030ff54trial   → trial   → dev → Applications

You will see two applications in the "dev" space:

 

STEP 5 — VERIFY THE HDI CONTAINER INSTANCE

Navigate to:

Space: dev → Services → Instances

You should see the HDI container service instance that was automatically created during deployment:

 This HDI container lives inside the SHC_DB HANA Cloud instance we created in Part 3. CAP automatically created it during the cf deploy step.

 

STEP 6 — EXPLORE THE HANA TABLES IN DB EXPLORER

Now let's verify the schema was deployed correctly by exploring the tables in SAP HANA Database Explorer.

Open HANA Cloud Central → click "Open" on the SHC_DB instance →
select "SAP HANA Database Explorer".

In Database Explorer, the left panel shows your SHC-DB instance connected as DBADMIN. To view the HDI container tables, click the "+" (Add Instance) icon.

 

Select "HDI Container" as the Instance Type, filter by your org (f030ff54trial) and space (dev).

Select "incident-management-db" and click OK.

 

The HDI container is now added to the left panel. Expand it to see the Tables folder. You will find all the CDS entities have been deployed as HANA tables.

Click on SAP_CAPIRE_INCIDENTS_INCIDENTS to inspect its structure:


STEP 7 — RUNNING IN HYBRID MODE

Now that the CAP app is deployed and the HDI container exists in BTP, you can run your local development server connected directly to the cloud HANA database. This is the "hybrid" mode — your app runs locally but reads and writes real HANA Cloud data.

This is very useful for testing local code changes against production data before deploying again.

Step 1 — Bind your local project to the HDI container service instance:

cds bind -2 incident-management-db:key

What this does:
- Connects to Cloud Foundry and retrieves the service key for the "incident-management-db" HDI container instance
- Creates a service key on BTP (if one doesn't exist)
- Saves the HANA connection credentials (host, schema, user, password) into .cdsrc-private.json under the "hybrid" profile
- The .cdsrc-private.json file is local only — never commit it to git

Step 2 — Start the server in hybrid mode:

cds watch --profile hybrid

The terminal confirms the app is now connected to the real HANA Cloud database, showing:
- The HANA host and schema ID
- Driver: com.sap.db.jdbc.Driver (JDBC connection to HANA)
- Both ProcessorService and AdminService are being served

Your app on localhost:4004 now reads and writes directly to the HANA Cloud instance in BTP — the Fiori preview will show and save real data.


SUMMARY

In Part 4, we successfully:

✔ Configured package.json with three CDS profiles: development (SQLite), hybrid (local + HANA Cloud), production (CF + HANA)
✔ Ran cds add hana to wire the project for HANA Cloud and generate mta.yaml
✔ Built the deployable MTA archive using mbt build
✔ Deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry using cf deploy
✔ Verified the two CF apps: incident-management-srv (Started) and incident-management-db-deployer (Stopped — expected)
✔ Confirmed the HDI container instance was created with 2 bindings
✔ Explored the HANA tables and seed data in SAP HANA Database Explorer
✔ Connected the local app to the HANA Cloud instance using hybrid mode

The CAP backend is now live on Cloud Foundry and backed by SAP HANA Cloud.
In Part 5 , we will build the Fiori Elements UI application and connect it to this deployed CAP service.
